Coming from somebody who works heavily in tracking data from high traffic environments, companies understand how much traffic they are going to get on peak days, and how large of a peak day this was going to be. They were hosting servers in AWS (I assume AWS East), which has the option of spinning up servers to meet the demand. Given that they understood how many people were given access codes, and could probably do the math on exactly how many hits it would take to get a denial of service, they had the option to pay for more AWS hosts to handle the load, but they didn't because they didn't want to pay for it. Whatever they had estimated, they should have factored in that multiple people were going to be refreshing, and many people are going to try to access the site anyway regardless of whether or not they had the code. They could have had the resources to confront this, but they didn't.

As I mentioned, I'm a professional software developer, and I'm also currently a computer science PhD student. My best friend and I both got presale codes, so we went through the presale experience together (each using our respective codes). My best friend is also a professional software developer working in high-level internet data management, and some of their work overlaps with the type of work done by Ticketmaster's software development/IT teams. In our professional opinions, we think people should be fired (not because someone has to take the blame, but because of incompetence). The issues with this sale were diverse, but many of them were caused by stupid mistakes made by Ticketmaster's software developers. It's clear to us that Ticketmaster's software developers were needlessly sloppy and failing to follow the basic best-practices of software development. Likely, there were also major miscommunications between Ticketmaster's sales strategy/software development/IT departments.

I am confused about the whole process: every single person I talked to had a very different experience. I kept hearing claims of price-gouging, but I don't see how that could be true. I was in line from 9 AM to 3 PM for tickets to the Detroit June 9 show. When I got in to select seats, the prices were exactly as I thought they'd be online: VIP were 799/899 for floor seats, etc. And there were still tix for $105 available. So everyone keeps talking about price gouging and dynamic pricing but I really didn't see that in my experience. Also, I wonder what criteria Ticketmaster used to send the presale codes: I think it has something to do with how much money you spent on Ticketmaster before. My wife and I both tried; her account is MUCH older than mine and she used to go to concerts almost weekly, and has spent a ton of money through TM. My account is younger/newer. She got a code; I didn't. We thought there might be an algorithm afoot that prioritizes TM big-spenders.

I think they should have made it so if you got a code you could get in. My friend didn’t get a code and she was trying to get tickets. I think they should have had a place before the queue to put in the code so people wouldn’t hold it up or go to get tickets and have no code. But someone got codes that didn’t work. And they should have made it clear to use the link they texted the night before.

I got assigned to my third choice location. The original form made it sound like we would most likely be able to purchase tickets at any of our 3 locations. I live 10 minutes away from the SoFi stadium in LA (my number 1 preference), but I was selected for Las Vegas my third choice. Most of my friends still live where my number 2 choice was, so I would have been happy with that one. But getting stuck with my third choice when so many other dates were released in my first 2 choices sucks. If they had all been announced when I was selecting dates originally, Vegas wouldn't have even been there. Yes I'm happy to even have tickets and at least it's only 5 hours away, but now I need a hotel and to convince people to travel with me to go...  It sucks.

Overall I really had no problems with my presale experience and I feel like I am a very very small minority of people who had zero issues. I never got a single error message or had any problems. At about 2:20 PM CT the queue started to move much quicker and I got in. I picked the first two seats I saw available together in a good section, spent about 4 minutes on the transaction page (not from loading, but checking with my dad about the seats via text) then went ahead and paid with no issues. I felt pretty confident going into the sale because I’ve participated in many of these before, and I had a boosted presale code based on previous purchases. This was an extremely stressful experience. I got in the waiting room for my venue right at 9:30 and joined the queue right at 10am. At 1005am, the queue disappeared and I had a notification that the sale was paused. I had to step away from my laptop to attend a work event around 12, so I left it with my boyfriend to stay queued while he was working, which ended up being an unexpected disruption to his day. The queue remained paused until 3pm, and after that point he waited about 30 mins in the queue to get to the ticket selection screen. It’s upsetting that my place in line realistically only had me in queue for 30 mins had the sale started when it was supposed to, and both of us ended up super stressed the entire day hoping not to disconnect and frustrated with the lack of transparency. From tracking other venues’ sales, it seemed like each venue’s queue was being opened up one at a time from East to West. When he finally got to the ticketing screen, he repeatedly tried to select several great options for tickets that appeared available, but was met with countless error messages. He continued trying for about 45 mins, and finally was able to secure tickets but they were an obstructed view and extremely expensive. Overall, I’m grateful that I will get to attend, especially since my friend is visiting from overseas and it’s our dream to see Taylor together. I had planned to try for better seats (so I could resell my tickets at face value to a fan) the next day for the Capital One presale and that one was just as frustrating for different reasons. Overall, it was a really stressful and disappointing experience.
The same tickets I had in my cart at $129 a piece and couldn’t buy are currently on stubhub for $1500 a piece. This is Gillette stadium. The bots and scalpers won.

Nobody owes anybody anything, this has all been a crazy lesson in entitlement for me.
